title: Isaiah 10
subtitle: Assyria: The Rod of God’s Anger

sections.header: Verses 1–19 — “I Send Him Against a Godless Nation”

heb.paragraph: "Woe to the Assyrian, the rod (maṭṭeh) of my anger, in whose hand is the club of my wrath! I send him against a godless nation" (vv.5–6). But Assyria does not intend this; in his heart he plans to destroy many nations (v.7). "Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?" (v.15). Assyria is God’s instrument but will be judged for exceeding its mandate. The remnant of Israel’s trees will be so few a child could count them (v.19).

refs.note: God’s response to Sennacherib’s arrogance: "Because you rage against me, I will put my hook in your nose" — the fulfilment of 10:5–19’s warning.
God raises the Babylonians as his instrument, just as he raised Assyria — the same pattern in a later generation.
I have given all these lands to Nebuchadnezzar my servant — the same instrumental-sovereignty theology applied to Babylon.
The potter’s sovereignty over the clay — Paul’s theological development of Isa 10’s instrumental logic.

notes.note: The seventh woe (continuing from 5:8–23): "Woe to those who make unjust laws." Legislation as injustice.
"Woe to the Assyrian, the rod of my anger!" God uses Assyria but Assyria exceeds the commission. The instrument is guilty.
"When the Lord finishes all his work against Mount Zion, he will say, I will punish the king of Assyria for the wilful pride of his heart." The tool will be judged for thinking it is the carpenter.
"Does the axe raise itself above the one who swings it?" The supreme statement of instrumental sovereignty. Tools don’t boast.

Calvin reads this as the key to all political theology: God uses nations as instruments without removing their moral responsibility. Assyria is both God’s rod AND a guilty empire. Both are simultaneously true.

Calvin: the axe analogy settles the question of sovereignty and freedom. The axe does not choose to cut — but if it could think, and it congratulated itself, it would be absurd. So is Assyria’s pride.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the "woe" on Assyria is structurally the seventh woe — completing the series begun in 5:8. The six woes on Judah are followed by a seventh on the instrument of judgment.
The NET provides four tool analogies: axe, saw, rod, club. Each is a variation on the same theme: the tool has no autonomous power. The repetition is deliberately exhaustive.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads this as the theological centre of First Isaiah’s political theology: God is sovereign over empires, but empires remain morally accountable. The two truths coexist without resolution.
Oswalt: the axe-question is the most rhetorically effective one-liner in the prophets. It reduces imperial arrogance to absurdity in a single sentence.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the Assyrian-rod passage as the canonical explanation for why exile happened (God sent the rod) and why the rod was broken (it exceeded its commission). The passage prevents both despair (God is in control) and fatalism (the instrument is judged).

Childs notes the tool-analogies function as a hermeneutical principle for reading all of Isaiah: every empire in the book (Assyria, Babylon, Persia) is simultaneously God’s instrument and God’s opponent.

hist.context: This passage is the OT’s fullest statement on instrumental sovereignty: God USES Assyria as his rod of judgment, but Assyria’s arrogance in exceeding the mandate brings judgment on the instrument itself. The "rod of anger" metaphor (v.5) makes Assyria a tool in God’s hand, not an independent actor. The axe/saw/rod/club analogies (v.15) drive the point home: tools do not congratulate themselves. Sennacherib’s boasts (vv.8–14) are historically documented in Assyrian royal inscriptions — the arrogance Isaiah describes is verbatim.

heb.paragraph: "A remnant (shəʾār) will return, a remnant of Jacob will return to the Mighty God" (v.21). In that day, Israel will no longer rely on the one who struck them (Assyria) but will truly rely on the LORD (v.20). The destruction decreed is overflowing with righteousness (v.22). Though your people be like the sand of the sea, only a remnant will return (v.22). Do not be afraid of the Assyrians (v.24). God will raise his staff over the waters as he did at the sea (v.26). The yoke will be broken (v.27). The chapter ends with Assyria’s march toward Jerusalem — place by place — stopped by God’s axe (vv.28–34).

refs.note: Isaiah cries out concerning Israel: "Though the number be as the sand of the sea, only the remnant will be saved" — Paul quotes Isa 10:22.
So too, at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace — Paul’s development of Isaiah’s remnant theology.
The angel of the LORD put to death 185,000 in the Assyrian camp — the fulfilment of the felling in 10:33–34.
God divided the sea — the exodus event 10:26 references: God will repeat the Red Sea for Assyria.

notes.note: A remnant will return to the Mighty God. But only a remnant. Paul quotes this in Rom 9:27–28. The promise is real but sobering.
"Do not be afraid of the Assyrians." God will raise his staff as at the sea. The exodus pattern repeats: what God did to Egypt, he will do to Assyria.
The Assyrian march: Aiath, Migron, Micmash, Geba, Ramah, Gibeah, Gallim, Laishah, Anathoth, Madmenah, Gebim, Nob. Place by place, closing in. Then stopped.
"The LORD will lop off the boughs with great power." The tall trees fall. The Assyrian advance is felled by God’s axe.
Calvin reads the remnant promise as simultaneously hopeful and devastating: survival is guaranteed, but survival is ONLY a remnant. God’s faithfulness preserves; God’s justice reduces.
Calvin sees the felling-of-trees as the setup for 11:1: the great trees of Assyria fall, and from the lowly stump of Jesse a shoot emerges. The mighty are brought low; the humble are raised.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "Mighty God" (ʾēl gibbōr) is the same title given to the child in 9:6. The remnant returns to the very figure who was promised. The theology is circular: the Messiah is the remnant’s destination.
The NET maps the place-names to an approach from the north, about 10 miles from Jerusalem. The geographic precision creates a sense of the noose tightening.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the remnant-return as the positive counterpart to 10:5–19’s judgment: the rod will be broken AND the remnant will return. Both happen because of the same divine sovereignty.
Oswalt: the tree-felling transitions directly to 11:1’s shoot-from-the-stump. The great forest of Assyria falls; the tiny shoot of Jesse rises. The chapter-break obscures the literary connection.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the remnant theology as the canonical thread that connects every section of Isaiah: 1:9 (survivors); 4:2–3 (the Branch); 6:13 (the stump); 7:3 (Shear-Jashub); 10:20–22 (the remnant returns). The remnant IS Isaiah’s hope.
Childs notes the transition from the felled forest (10:34) to the sprouting stump (11:1) is the most important chapter-break in Isaiah: destruction gives way to the messianic king.

hist.context: The Shear-Jashub prophecy (7:3) is fulfilled here: "a remnant will return." Paul quotes v.22 in Romans 9:27–28 for his theology of the remnant within Israel. The place-names in vv.28–32 trace an actual approach-route to Jerusalem from the north, giving the poem a reportorial immediacy. The final image (v.33–34) is God as lumberjack felling the tall trees — the Assyrian advance halted by divine intervention, fulfilled when the angel kills 185,000 in Sennacherib’s camp (37:36).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVto deprive the poor of their rights and withhold justice from the oppressed of my people, making widows their prey and robbing the fatherless.ESVto turn aside the needy from justice and to rob the poor of my people of their right, that widows may be their spoil, and that they may make the fatherless their prey!NIVWoe to those who make unjust laws, to those who issue oppressive decrees,ESVWoe to those who decree iniquitous decrees, and the writers who keep writing oppression,

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: The Cyrus Cylinder relates to Deutero-Isaiah (chapters 40-55), not Isaiah chapter 1 being discussed here.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: The Sennacherib Prism relates to events described in Isaiah 36-39, not Isaiah chapter 1.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: Isaiah 10:1 ('Woe to those who make unjust laws') is not a continuation of the woes in Isaiah 5:8-23, but begins a new prophetic oracle against social injustice.

rows.label: The Rod of God’s Anger — 10:1–19
rows.text: "Woe to the Assyrian, the ROD of my anger!" "Does the AXE raise itself above the one who swings it?"

hebtext.note: "Woe to the Assyrian, the rod (maṭṭeh) of my anger, in whose hand is the club of my wrath! I send him against a godless nation" (vv.5–6). But Assyria does not intend this; in his heart he plans to destroy many nations (v.7). "Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?" (v.15). Assyria is God’s instrument but will be judged for exceeding its mandate. The remnant of Israel’s trees will be so few a child could count them (v.19).

"A remnant (shəʾār) will return, a remnant of Jacob will return to the Mighty God" (v.21). In that day, Israel will no longer rely on the one who struck them (Assyria) but will truly rely on the LORD (v.20). The destruction decreed is overflowing with righteousness (v.22). Though your people be like the sand of the sea, only a remnant will return (v.22). Do not be afraid of the Assyrians (v.24). God will raise his staff over the waters as he did at the sea (v.26). The yoke will be broken (v.27). The chapter ends with Assyria’s march toward Jerusalem — place by place — stopped by God’s axe (vv.28–34).

thread.text: God’s response to Sennacherib’s arrogance: "Because you rage against me, I will put my hook in your nose" — the fulfilment of 10:5–19’s warning.
God raises the Babylonians as his instrument, just as he raised Assyria — the same pattern in a later generation.
I have given all these lands to Nebuchadnezzar my servant — the same instrumental-sovereignty theology applied to Babylon.
The potter’s sovereignty over the clay — Paul’s theological development of Isa 10’s instrumental logic.
Isaiah cries out concerning Israel: "Though the number be as the sand of the sea, only the remnant will be saved" — Paul quotes Isa 10:22.
So too, at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace — Paul’s development of Isaiah’s remnant theology.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: God uses and judges empires simultaneously. The axe/rod/saw metaphor. Judgment and Justice at 8: the rod exceeds its mandate.

coaching.tip: Assyria is God's instrument — 'the rod of my anger' (v. 5) — but doesn't know it. The king of Assyria thinks he conquers by his own power (vv. 8–14). God uses him, then judges him for the arrogance the mission revealed. This creates a paradox at the heart of Isaiah's theology: God employs nations as tools while holding them accountable for their motives. Sovereignty and responsibility coexist.

'A remnant will return' (v. 21) — Shear-Jashub, Isaiah's son's name from chapter 7 — becomes theological doctrine here. The remnant concept threads through the entire Old Testament: Noah's family, Abraham's line, the 7,000 who didn't bow to Baal. Paul picks it up in Romans 11:5. It's God's pattern: not total destruction, not total preservation, but a faithful core through which the story continues.
